title: Algorithmic Learning in a Random World
authors: Vladimir Vovk, Alexander Gammerman, Glenn Shafer
summary: The definitive textbook on conformal prediction. Covers set prediction (conformal predictors for regression and classification, inductive/cross-conformal/Mondrian variants), probabilistic prediction (Venn predictors, conformal predictive systems), testing randomness, and online compression modelling. The only assumption required for validity is exchangeability. The 2nd edition adds three new chapters on conformal predictive distributions, efficiency of conformal testing, and using conformal testing to protect against distribution shifts.
relevance: Foundational reference for all conformal prediction methodology. Provides the theoretical underpinnings for split conformal prediction, which is directly applicable to constructing prediction intervals for node displacements and beam element forces in the surrogate model. The exchangeability assumption and its relaxations are critical when dealing with graph-structured structural data where samples may share topological dependencies.

title: A Tutorial on Conformal Prediction
summary: A self-contained tutorial introducing conformal prediction as a framework for producing prediction sets with guaranteed coverage under minimal assumptions (exchangeability). Designed for an online setting where labels are predicted sequentially. Introduces the concept of a nonconformity measure. Includes worked numerical examples on the Iris dataset. Builds on concepts from the authors' 2005 book.
relevance: The standard introductory reference for conformal prediction. Useful for understanding how to construct nonconformity measures for regression tasks (node displacement and beam force prediction) and how split conformal prediction works in practice. Provides the basis for constructing prediction intervals without distributional assumptions.

title: Conformal Prediction: A Gentle Introduction
venue: Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning
summary: A comprehensive, accessible monograph on conformal prediction covering both methodology and practice. Topics include split conformal prediction, weighted/full conformal prediction, conformalized quantile regression, and applications to modern ML. Focuses on distribution-free, finite-sample validity. The 2021 arXiv preprint (arXiv:2107.07511) is widely cited as 'A Gentle Introduction to Conformal Prediction and Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification'.
relevance: The most practical and modern introduction to conformal prediction. Directly relevant for implementing split conformal prediction for the structural surrogate model. The coverage of conformalized quantile regression (CQR) is particularly useful for handling heteroscedasticity in displacement/force predictions, where prediction intervals should be wider for high-response regions.

title: Distribution-Free Predictive Inference for Regression
authors: Jing Lei, Max G'Sell, Alessandro Rinaldo, Ryan J. Tibshirani, Larry Wasserman
venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
summary: A cornerstone paper on conformal prediction for regression. Provides a unified framework for distribution-free predictive inference, including split conformal prediction, jackknife methods, and cross-conformal prediction. Establishes finite-sample coverage guarantees without distributional assumptions. All authors from Department of Statistics, Carnegie Mellon University.
relevance: Highly relevant for establishing the theoretical validity of conformal prediction intervals for the regression tasks in the surrogate model (node displacement prediction and beam internal force prediction). The split conformal method described here is the most practical approach for the structural engineering setting, as it requires only a single trained model and a calibration set.

title: Distribution-Free Prediction Bands for Non-parametric Regression
venue: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B
summary: Studies distribution-free, nonparametric prediction bands with a focus on finite-sample behavior. Introduces the COPS (Conformal Optimized Prediction Set) estimator, which combines conformal prediction with nonparametric conditional density estimation. Proves that distribution-free conditional coverage is impossible with finite samples, motivating 'local validity' as an intermediate concept. The COPS method converges to an oracle band at minimax optimal rate.
relevance: Provides theoretical foundations for understanding the limits of conditional coverage in conformal prediction. Important for the structural surrogate application, where conditional coverage per region (e.g., support zones, mid-span) may be more meaningful than marginal coverage. The concept of 'local validity' could inform region-aware conformal prediction strategies for steel truss girders.

title: Inductive Confidence Machines for Regression
authors: Harris Papadopoulos, Kostas Proedrou, Volodya Vovk, Alexander Gammerman
bibtex:
@inproceedings{papadopoulos2002inductive,
  author    = {Harris Papadopoulos and Kostas Proedrou and Volodya Vovk and Alexander Gammerman},
  title     = {Inductive Confidence Machines for Regression},
  booktitle = {Machine Learning: ECML 2002},
  series    = {LNCS},
  volume    = {2430},
  pages     = {345--356},
  year      = {2002},
  publisher = {Springer},
  doi       = {10.1007/3-540-36755-1_29}
}
summary: Introduces the Inductive Confidence Machine (ICM), replacing the previous transductive inference approach with inductive (split) inference for regression problems. The algorithm is based on Ridge Regression and is significantly faster than transductive techniques, making it suitable for large datasets. This is the seminal paper that introduced the split/inductive conformal prediction approach that dominates modern practice.
relevance: Foundational paper for split conformal prediction, which is the most practical method for the structural surrogate model. The inductive approach avoids the computational burden of retraining for each test point, making it feasible for large-scale structural datasets with many nodes and elements.

title: Regression Conformal Prediction with Nearest Neighbours
authors: Harris Papadopoulos, Vladimir Vovk, Alexander Gammerman
summary: Applies conformal prediction to k-nearest neighbours regression with six novel nonconformity measures. Develops both transductive and inductive (split) CP approaches. Shows significant improvement in predictive region tightness over the standard regression nonconformity measure.
relevance: Demonstrates how different nonconformity measures affect prediction interval efficiency. The concept of designing task-specific nonconformity measures is directly applicable to the structural surrogate setting, where measures could incorporate physical constraints or structural features to produce tighter intervals.

title: Predictive Inference with the Jackknife+
authors: Rina Foygel Barber, Emmanuel J. Candes, Aaditya Ramdas, Ryan J. Tibshirani
summary: Introduces the jackknife+ method for constructing predictive confidence intervals. Unlike the classic jackknife, it uses leave-one-out predictions at the test point, achieving a rigorous distribution-free coverage guarantee (at least 1-2alpha) under exchangeability. Extends naturally to CV+ with K-fold cross-validation. Under algorithmic stability, achieves near target 1-alpha coverage.
relevance: Provides an alternative to split conformal prediction that uses the training data more efficiently. Useful for the structural surrogate model when the training set is limited (70 samples). The CV+ variant could be particularly valuable for making the most of limited structural engineering datasets.

title: Conformalized Quantile Regression
authors: Yaniv Romano, Evan Patterson, Emmanuel J. Candes
venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2019)
booktitle: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32
summary: Proposes Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR), combining conformal prediction with quantile regression to inherit the advantages of both: finite-sample, distribution-free coverage guarantees and adaptive interval width for heteroscedastic data. Evaluated on 11 benchmark datasets with quantile random forests and neural networks. Consistently produces shorter intervals than standard split conformal prediction while maintaining valid coverage.
relevance: Directly applicable to structural surrogate modeling. Displacement and force predictions exhibit heteroscedasticity (e.g., larger variance at high-response regions). CQR would produce prediction intervals that adapt to the varying uncertainty levels across different structural regions (support zones, mid-span, connection zones), making it superior to fixed-width intervals.

title: Conformal Inductive Graph Neural Networks
summary: Addresses conformal prediction for inductive (unseen) nodes in GNNs. Standard CP requires exchangeability, which fails in inductive settings due to score shifts from message passing with new nodes. Proposes NodeEx CP and EdgeEx CP frameworks that recover coverage guarantees under node- or edge-exchangeability assumptions. Coverage guarantee holds independently of prediction time.
relevance: Highly relevant for the structural surrogate GNN. The inductive setting is analogous to making predictions for new structural designs (unseen samples in by_sample split). The paper's treatment of how graph structure affects exchangeability and coverage guarantees is critical for valid uncertainty quantification in the heterogeneous physical graph.

title: Residual Reweighted Conformal Prediction for Graph Neural Networks
authors: Zheng Zhang, Jie Bao, Zhixin Zhou, Nicolo Colombo, Lixin Cheng, Rui Luo
venue: Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI 2025)
summary: Proposes RR-GNN for minimal prediction sets with provable marginal coverage on graphs. Key innovations: (1) Graph-Structured Mondrian CP that partitions nodes into communities using topological features for cluster-conditional coverage; (2) Residual-Adaptive Nonconformity Scores using a secondary GNN to estimate residuals; (3) Cross-Training Protocol preventing information leakage. Validated on 15 real-world graphs for node regression, classification, and edge weight prediction. Reduces prediction-interval inefficiency by 6%-29%.
relevance: State-of-the-art method for conformal prediction on graph node regression. The Graph-Structured Mondrian CP is directly applicable to providing region-aware coverage guarantees for the structural surrogate, where nodes can be partitioned by structural zones (support, mid-span, connection). The residual-adaptive approach addresses heteroscedasticity in structural responses.

title: Conformal Prediction for Network-Assisted Regression
authors: Robert Lunde, Elizaveta Levina, Ji Zhu
venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
summary: Shows that under joint exchangeability, a network analog of conformal prediction achieves finite-sample validity for node attribute prediction. Applicable to node regression using both network covariates (graph embeddings, local subgraph counts) and conventional node covariates. Provides theoretical framework for CP on network data.
relevance: Directly relevant as it establishes the theoretical validity of conformal prediction for node-level regression in network/graph data. The joint exchangeability framework can be adapted to the heterogeneous physical graph where nodes (mesh nodes, beam elements, plate elements) have complex dependency structures. Provides the theoretical justification for applying split conformal prediction to the surrogate model's node predictions.

title: A Hybrid Data-Physics Framework with Conformal GNN for Enhanced Damage Identification
authors: Armin Dadras Eslamlou, Arshia Ghasemlou, Brais Barros, Belen Riveiro
summary: Proposes a hybrid framework integrating GNN with physics-based FE model updating for structural damage identification. A GNN trained on modal data from FE simulations estimates damage location and severity, with AutoML (GA) optimizing architecture. Conformal prediction at 90% confidence quantifies uncertainty in GNN predictions, which then warm-start FE model updating via Water Strider Algorithm. Validated on Louisville bridge (planar truss), IASC-ASCE building, and a dome structure.
relevance: Extremely relevant as it directly applies conformal prediction + GNN to structural engineering (truss bridge). Demonstrates the feasibility of using conformal prediction for uncertainty quantification in structural surrogate models. The truss bridge application (Louisville bridge) is particularly analogous to the steel truss girder in this project. The hybrid physics+data approach provides a template for incorporating physics into CP-based UQ.

title: Calibrated Physics-Informed Uncertainty Quantification
authors: Vignesh Gopakumar, Ander Gray, Lorenzo Zanisi, Timothy Nunn, Daniel Giles, Matt J. Kusner, Stanislas Pamela, Marc Peter Deisenroth
booktitle: Proceedings of the 42nd International Conference on Machine Learning
summary: Introduces CP-PRE (Conformal Prediction with Physics Residual Error), a model-agnostic, physics-informed conformal prediction framework providing guaranteed uncertainty estimates without requiring labelled data. Uses PDE residuals as nonconformity scores and convolutional layers as finite-difference stencils. Validated on neural PDE models for plasma modelling and fusion reactor design. Offers both marginal and joint coverage guarantees.
relevance: Highly innovative approach that replaces ground-truth labels with PDE residuals as nonconformity scores. For the structural surrogate, this suggests a pathway to using physics residuals (e.g., equilibrium residuals, compatibility residuals) as nonconformity measures, enabling physics-constrained UQ even in regions with limited ground-truth data. The data-free CP framework could be adapted to use structural mechanics PDE residuals.

title: Conformal Load Prediction with Transductive Graph Autoencoders
summary: Combines graph autoencoders (GAEs) with conformal prediction for edge weight regression (load prediction on graph edges). Handles heteroscedasticity via error reweighting and Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR). Demonstrates valid prediction intervals on graph-structured data where edge-level predictions are the target.
relevance: Relevant for the beam element force prediction task in the structural surrogate, where beam element forces are edge-level or element-level predictions. The error reweighting approach could be adapted to handle the varying uncertainty across different structural elements. The transductive GAE framework may inform uncertainty quantification for the heterogeneous physical graph.

title: Uncertainty Quantification of a Machine Learning Model for Identification of Isolated Nonlinearities With Conformal Prediction
authors: David Najera-Flores, Derek Jacobs, Dane Quinn, Anthony Garland, Michael Todd
summary: Applies conformal prediction for UQ of a physics-informed (structure-preserving) neural network used to identify localized nonlinearities in structural systems. Uses conformal sets to quantify sensitivity to measurement noise and deploys in a structural health monitoring (SHM) framework to detect deviations from baseline state.
relevance: Directly applies conformal prediction to structural systems with physics-informed neural networks. Demonstrates the practical viability of CP for engineering UQ. The SHM context provides a template for how conformal prediction intervals can be used to detect structural changes or anomalies in truss girder predictions.

title: Distribution Free Prediction Sets for Node Classification
summary: Proposes Neighbourhood Adaptive Prediction Sets (NAPS), extending conformal prediction to node classification by weighting conformal scores based on graph structure. Leverages non-exchangeable conformal prediction (Barber et al., 2022) and homophily to produce tighter, better-calibrated prediction sets for graph nodes.
relevance: Demonstrates a structure-aware approach to conformal prediction on graphs that weights nonconformity scores by graph topology. The neighborhood-adaptive concept could be extended to the structural surrogate by weighting prediction intervals based on structural connectivity (e.g., nodes in the same load path share similar uncertainty levels).

title: A Gentle Introduction to Conformal Prediction and Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification
summary: The widely-cited arXiv preprint version of the 2023 Foundations and Trends monograph. Provides an accessible introduction to conformal prediction with a focus on modern applications, including split conformal prediction, CQR, weighted conformal prediction, and adaptive prediction sets. Emphasizes distribution-free, finite-sample guarantees and practical implementation.
relevance: Same as the 2023 monograph but with open-access availability. Useful as a practical implementation reference for building conformal prediction intervals for the structural surrogate model. The algorithmic descriptions and code examples are directly translatable to the project's Python implementation.
